```{r}
# Install forcats if not already installed
# install.packages("forcats")

# Load libraries
library(ggplot2)
library(forcats)


# Data
transp <- c("bycicle", "bus", "bus", "walking", "bus", "bycicle", "bycicle",
            "bus", "bus", "bus" , "bycicle", "bus", "bycicle", "bycicle", 
            "walking", "bus", "bus", "bicycle", "bicycle", "walking", "walking",
            "bicycle", "bus", "bus", "bus", "bus", "bicycle", "bus", "bus",
            "bicycle", "bicycle", "bicycle")
dat1 <- data.frame(transp)

# Graph
# ggplot() initializes the plot object; data=dat1 specifies the data source
ggplot(data=dat1) +
  # geom_bar() adds a bar layer; mapping=aes() defines how data maps to visuals
  # fct_infreq(transp) reorders the categories of 'transp' by their frequency
  # geom_bar(mapping=aes(x=(transp))) +
  geom_bar(mapping=aes(x=fct_infreq(transp))) +
  # xlab() sets the label for the x-axis
  xlab("Transportations")

```

* ggplot(data=dat1):
  * This initializes the plot.
  * data=dat1 tells R to use your dataframe dat1 as the source of truth for the visualization.

* +:
  * In ggplot2, the plus sign is used to "layer" components on top of each other. You start with a base plot and then add layers (geoms, scales, labels).

* geom_bar():
  * This specifies the geometric object to use. geom_bar specifically creates bars where the height is proportional to the number of cases in each group (frequency).
* mapping=aes(x=...):
  * aes (Aesthetic mapping) defines how variables in your data are mapped to visual properties of the plot.
  * Here, we map the x-axis to your transportation data.
* fct_infreq(transp):
  * This is a function from the forcats package.
  * By default, ggplot orders categorical data alphabetically. fct_infreq reorders the categories of transp based on their frequency (count), so the most frequent transportation method appears first on the left.
* xlab("Transportations"):
  * This simply sets a custom user-friendly label for the X-axis.
